content.text: A review process with no time commitment is just a wishlist. Service-level agreements (SLAs) define the maximum elapsed time between a PR being ready for review and a reviewer responding. Ownership rules define who is responsible for reviewing what. Together they transform 'we have a review process' into 'we have a review process that actually runs in time to meet release schedules.'

attrs.alt: Review SLA timeline: PR opens at T+0, CI gates pass around T+2h, standard SLA expires at T+3 business days, escalation triggers at T+5 days. Security-expedited SLA is 2 hours total.

content.text: Not all PRs have the same urgency. Tiering the SLA by change type prevents low-priority patches from clogging the queue while ensuring security fixes move quickly:
Minor or patch prompt change; code-only change with no prompt file diff
Major version bump, new template, capability scope expansion
Active security vulnerability in a live prompt; injection confirmed in production
Security reviewer (1 approval, prompt lead notified)
Metadata update, changelog entry, comment fix — no system prompt text changed
SLA clocks start when all CI gates pass, not when the PR is opened. A PR that is still failing gate 2 is not yet ready for review, so the SLA has not begun.

CODEOWNERS determines who is automatically added as a required reviewer. Structure the file to mirror the prompt directory hierarchy — broad ownership at the top, escalating specificity for higher-risk directories:

# .github/CODEOWNERS
# Default: all prompt changes require the prompt leads team
prompts/                           @acme/prompt-leads

# Customer-facing and tool-use prompts also require security review
prompts/customer-facing/           @acme/prompt-leads @acme/security-reviewers
prompts/tool-use/                  @acme/prompt-leads @acme/security-reviewers

# Templates that access PII additionally require data-privacy review
prompts/customer-facing/pii-scope/ @acme/prompt-leads @acme/security-reviewers @acme/data-privacy

# Each product team owns their subdirectory (informational, not blocking)
prompts/billing/                   @acme/prompt-leads @acme/billing-team
prompts/support/                   @acme/prompt-leads @acme/support-eng
All prompt template changes: identity, capability scope, output format, safety baseline version, eval suite coverage
Blocking — PR cannot merge without approval
Injection resistance findings, capability boundary strength, PII handling in context injection, output scanner configuration
Blocking for customer-facing and tool-use prompts
Whether PII is processed, whether appropriate data retention limits are set, whether audit events cover all personal data flows
Business logic correctness, accuracy of capability descriptions, alignment with product requirements
Informational — cannot merge without prompt lead, but product team member can request changes that prompt lead enforces
SLA monitoring should be automated. A GitHub Actions workflow can query open PRs labelled 'prompt-change' and alert when the SLA deadline passes without a review:
When automation detects a breach, the escalation ladder determines who is contacted and in what order:
Automated Slack alert to #prompt-reviews channel with PR link and tier.
Direct message to the assigned reviewer(s). If the reviewer is the prompt lead, also message the backup prompt lead.
Alert to the engineering manager of the team that owns the PR. The PR is also flagged in the weekly review sync agenda.
Page the on-call security engineer. Security-expedited SLA breaches are treated as incidents.
The prompt leads team should maintain a weekly rotation so no single person is the sole bottleneck. The rotation schedule defines a primary and secondary reviewer for each week:

# .github/prompt-review-rotation.yml
# Week-of date (ISO 8601 Monday) -> reviewer assignment
rotation:
  "2025-07-07":
    primary: "@alice"
    secondary: "@bob"
  "2025-07-14":
    primary: "@carol"
    secondary: "@alice"
  "2025-07-21":
    primary: "@bob"
    secondary: "@carol"

# Security reviewer rotation (separate from prompt lead rotation)
security_rotation:
  "2025-07-07":
    primary: "@dave"
    secondary: "@erin"
  "2025-07-14":
    primary: "@erin"
    secondary: "@frank"

# Out-of-office overrides (always check this before the rotation)
out_of_office:
  "@alice": { from: "2025-07-10", to: "2025-07-14", coverage: "@carol" }

When a reviewer is on planned leave, the backup must be designated at least two business days in advance. The reviewer informs the team in the #prompt-reviews channel and updates the out_of_office section of the rotation file in a PR. The coverage reviewer receives elevated GitHub notification level for the duration of the absence.

Assigning a label at PR creation time is the trigger for SLA tracking. The author is responsible for adding the correct label; CI validates that a recognised label is present on any PR that touches the prompts directory:
Track the following metrics monthly to detect whether the review process is meeting its commitments:
fraction of PRs that received a first response within the SLA. Target ≥ 95% for standard tier, 100% for security-expedited.
median and 95th-percentile time from CI passing to first review comment. Rising P95 signals queue buildup before SLA breach.
fraction of PRs that triggered an escalation alert. Target < 5%. Above 10% indicates the rotation is understaffed.
fraction of all reviews done by the single busiest reviewer. Target < 40%. Higher values indicate single-point-of-failure risk.
time PRs sit awaiting review as a fraction of total PR cycle time. Target < 50%. If most of the delay is review wait rather than author iteration, the review team is the bottleneck.

GitHub's default email notification volume is high enough that reviewers often develop 'notification blindness' — they filter out review requests alongside issue activity and CI updates. Dedicated configuration reduces noise while ensuring prompt-change review requests stand out:

# Recommended GitHub notification settings for prompt lead reviewers
# (configured per-user at github.com/settings/notifications)

# 1. Enable 'Review requested' notifications for email AND web
#    (Settings → Notifications → Participating, @mentions and custom → Review requested)

# 2. Subscribe to the prompts/ directory via a watch filter:
#    Go to the repository → Watch → Custom → uncheck Everything, check 'Pull requests'
#    Then use the GitHub CLI to add a directory watch:

# gh api /repos/OWNER/REPO/subscription -X PUT -f subscribed=true

A GitHub Actions step can post a formatted Slack message when a PR that requires prompt lead review is opened, ensuring the notification reaches the team's primary communication channel rather than relying on email:
When a reviewer leaves the team
Reviewer turnover is a realistic scenario and must not leave ownership gaps. The handoff procedure ensures no prompt template becomes orphaned:
Two weeks before the reviewer's last day, they identify a replacement and begin a shadow-review period — the replacement reviews all incoming PRs in parallel.
The departing reviewer updates CODEOWNERS to replace their GitHub handle with the replacement's, submitting it as a standard PR (prompt lead approves the CODEOWNERS change as well).
The departing reviewer updates the rotation schedule YAML with the replacement's handle for all future weeks they were scheduled.
Any open PRs they are assigned to review are reassigned via the PR page; the replacement is added as a required reviewer and the departing reviewer is removed.
A 'prompt-ownership-transfer' issue is filed recording which templates changed hands and the date, for audit trail purposes.

A CODEOWNERS change is itself a prompt-adjacent change that can affect who reviews future security-critical PRs. It should always be approved by both the outgoing and incoming reviewer (or the prompt lead if the outgoing reviewer is already gone) rather than being merged unilaterally.

Certain conditions may justify a temporary SLA waiver — an extended team holiday, a critical production incident consuming all reviewer bandwidth, or an external event causing a security-expedited review queue to spike. Waivers are not self-granted:
Duration of the holiday plus 1 business day
Active P1 incident consuming reviewer bandwidth
Duration of the incident plus 4 hours
Until coverage is arranged (max 2 business days)
Slack message in #prompt-reviews naming the backup
Team calendar block + advance announcement (1 week minimum)

Even with a waiver, security-expedited PRs must still be reviewed — if the primary security reviewer is unavailable, the waiver must name the specific on-call backup who will cover security-expedited SLAs during the waiver period.

Review capacity must be treated as a sprint commitment, not an ad-hoc obligation. Including review hours in sprint planning prevents the common failure mode where developers schedule too many feature PRs and reviewers have no protected time to review them:
the prompt lead role carries a 20% sprint velocity reduction to account for review time. Do not assign the prompt lead a full sprint's worth of feature work.
at the sprint planning meeting, each team announces which prompt changes they expect to submit. Reviewers can pre-plan their review schedule rather than reacting to an unpredictable queue.
if a PR was not reviewed within SLA in a given sprint, it automatically becomes the first review task in the next sprint for the primary reviewer — it is not deprioritised again.
track the number of prompt PRs reviewed per sprint per reviewer. A sudden drop signals that the reviewer is overloaded with other work and may need temporary backup coverage.

Review SLAs and ownership rules operationalise the review process by answering two questions: who reviews, and by when. Tiered SLAs (standard 3 business days, security-expedited 2 hours) align review urgency with change risk. CODEOWNERS enforces the correct reviewers automatically; the escalation ladder ensures breaches trigger a human response rather than silently expiring. Rotation schedules prevent individual burnout and single-point-of-failure risk. SLA compliance metrics close the loop by making the process measurable and improvable over time.
SLA tiers map to change risk: patch-only < standard < major < security-expedited.
SLA clocks start when all CI gates pass, not at PR open time.
CODEOWNERS blocks merge without the correct approvers for each directory.
The escalation ladder triggers Slack alerts, DMs, and pages in sequence as the breach ages.
Rotation schedules distribute review load and must be updated at least two business days before planned absences.
Compliance is measured monthly; escalation rate > 10% or reviewer concentration > 40% signals a structural problem.
